content:
Is there anyone working on 32-bit ARM?
If so what are the blockers for this?
We had some initial arm32 support in the old rust branch, in case it helps.

I haven't found any more recent work in it, is that just because none is interested in doing the work or are there any known blockers?
I've experimented a bit and got a example module to build, but did not have time to try it on HW.

There has been some interest in support for other ARM architectures, but I don't think anybody has looked into it too much. See some discussion at https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20231020155056.3495121-1-Jamie.Cunliffe@arm.com/T/#m38371377bc9b5a217aeab4b2b93a4ac23b7c70c6 where a big endian ARM target was brought up
32-bit x86 is blocked on a couple specific things but I don't know if those apply to ARM https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/288089-General/topic/x86.2032-bit.20support
If you are able to verify that what you have works on hardware, you may as well send a patch to at least get the discussion rolling. It would look similar to that first link I sent

I haven't found any more recent work in it, is that just because none is interested in doing the work or are there any known blockers?

Possibly the former. The support we had in arm worked and booted (in QEMU) for a given config/CPU as a proof of concept (armv6). It would be great if someone interested in ARM can review/extend the support if needed, test it with actual hardware (as Trevor said) and submit it upstream (ideally with several configs/variations, say, BE vs. LE).

32-bit x86 is blocked on a couple specific things but I don't know if those apply to ARM

If you mean regparm and reg-struct-return, I don't think they are used in arm32. But it could very well be that other arch-specific flags are needed for particular configs that rustc may not support yet, as you say. It would already be very welcome work if someone can take a look and have an updated report on what may be missing specifically for arm32 (and create/link the issues in/to upstream Rust).

I've got it to work locally and have tested out loading/unloading the sample modules in qemu.
When running the kunit test I've noticed that one test fails:

[16:56:50] # DEFINE_FLEX_test: EXPECTATION FAILED at lib/overflow_kunit.c:1188
[16:56:50] Expected __builtin_dynamic_object_size(two, 0) == sizeof(struct foo) + sizeof(s16) + sizeof(s16), but
[16:56:50]     __builtin_dynamic_object_size(two, 0) == 8 (0x8)
[16:56:50]     sizeof(struct foo) + sizeof(s16) + sizeof(s16) == 12 (0xc)
[16:56:50] [FAILED] DEFINE_FLEX_test
[16:56:50]     # module: overflow_kunit
[16:56:50] # overflow: pass:21 fail:1 skip:0 total:22
[16:56:50] # Totals: pass:21 fail:1 skip:0 total:22

but that also failes without my patch, so I think I can ignore this (maybe because of LLVM=1, did not investigate this more).

How should I continue with this and is there even any interest in having this upstreamed?
I've got the source code for this here: https://github.com/onestacked/linux/commit/4e938f351f705ea34177d523d33cccfdc0273b1e

Currently I've used define_panicking_intrinsics! to "implement" the __eabi_ f32 and f64 functions as well as __aeabi_uldivmod(64 bit Division with remainder) .
I'm not sure if there should be a soft implementation for __aeabi_uldivmod instead as the rust core library uses this intrinsic.
5 messages were moved from this topic to #General > Kunit failure with ARCH=arm and LLVM=1 by Christian Schrefl.

I have now tested this on hardware. I've used a Terrasic DE1soc board (Altera FPGA + arm cortex A9 CPU).
I've tested the rust samples and a custom module that just writes to some address to turn on a led throgh the FPGA part of the chip.

Should I send my patches in now or should I wait for the next merge window?

That is great!

Please feel free to send them at any time to the arm subsystem and us. Please also Cc Jamie that sent the arm64 support (https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20231020155056.3495121-1-Jamie.Cunliffe@arm.com/).

In addition, @Sven Van Asbroeck added the initial arm32 support (when we were out-of-tree) in 51ea67e0d5d4 and related commits -- I don't know using Co-developed-by makes sense here or not, or a mention out of deference, etc., but just in case I thought I would mention it.

Thanks!

Sent it in here:
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/4e0f5932-c7bc-4878-862c-1186cbecd71d@gmail.com/
Thanks! Replying in the list.

I've sent in a V2 about two weeks ago and only received one reply, did this just get overlooked, or did I do something wrong?
Link to lore: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/2dbd1491-149d-443c-9802-75786a6a3b73@gmail.com/T/
Probably just nobody has gotten to it, you can reply to your own email with a gentle nudge

Regarding the missing symbols, we might be able to just use vendor in the asm part from compiler_builtins, https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/blob/7a84ce4a90cd2d22494daead50f9026b5ca52140/src/arm.rs#L39. Requires __udivmoddi4 (unless we just vendor the whole thing), but seems like that one might be available?
Down the line that is, not for initial support
Another thing - if you could get some TBs or RBs from others who have hardware or are familiar with the platform (RfL members or just people you know from work or whatever), that helps show it works

Trevor Gross said:

Regarding the missing symbols, we might be able to just use vendor in the asm part from compiler_builtins, https://github.com/rust-lang/compiler-builtins/blob/7a84ce4a90cd2d22494daead50f9026b5ca52140/src/arm.rs#L39. Requires __udivmoddi4 (unless we just vendor the whole thing), but seems like that one might be available?

in arch/arm/nwfpe/Makefile there is a commen:

nwfpe-$(CONFIG_FPE_NWFPE_XP)    += extended_cpdo.o

# Try really hard to avoid generating calls to __aeabi_uldivmod() from
# float64_rem() due to loop elision.
ifdef CONFIG_CC_IS_CLANG
CFLAGS_softfloat.o  += -mllvm -replexitval=never
endif

So I think we don't want to provide __aeabi_uldivmod.

At least until we have a good reason for it.
Also @Jamie Cunliffe who may want to know about this thread.
